schools.summary: Gill: the outcry began with the princes and elders with Moses but quickly spread through the body of the people, very loud and clamorous, with shrieks, tears, and lamentations.
Henry: the people wept that night, giving way to despair at the report of the spies.

themes: all the congregation lifted up their voice, and cried; and the people wept that night
a night of useless tears
grieving for fear, not for sin

schools.summary: Gill: they murmured against Moses and Aaron as the instruments of bringing them out of Egypt and conducting them hither.
Keil & Delitzsch: the whole congregation said, 'Would God that we had died in the land of Egypt, or in this wilderness.'

themes: all the children of Israel murmured... Would God that we had died in... Egypt... or... this wilderness
preferring the grave to the inheritance
their wish to die there granted

schools.summary: Gill: their murmuring did not stop at Moses and Aaron but proceeded against God himself, who had done such wonderful things for them, complaining of his favours as if they were injuries, as if God had brought them out of Egypt only to destroy them.

themes: wherefore hath the LORD brought us unto this land, to fall by the sword... were it not better... to return into Egypt?
construing God's mercies as malice
preferring bondage to trusting him

schools.summary: Gill: they would make a head over them instead of Moses, who would never take them back and to whom they were now disaffected; Nehemiah says they did appoint a captain (Neh. 9:17), to return into Egypt.

themes: Let us make a captain, and let us return into Egypt
casting off Moses and God's leading
returning to bondage rather than follow God into rest

schools.summary: Gill: done through shame and confusion at so shocking a proposal and such ingratitude, and to beg the people to lay aside such thoughts and to supplicate God to convince them of their sin and give them repentance and forgiveness.

themes: Moses and Aaron fell on their faces before all the assembly
grieved and praying mediators
interceding while the congregation raged

schools.summary: Gill: they rose up and interposed, looking on themselves as specially obliged, being able to confront the other spies and contradict what they had said.
Keil & Delitzsch: Joshua and Caleb, of them that searched the land, rent their clothes.

themes: Joshua the son of Nun, and Caleb the son of Jephunneh... rent their clothes
the faithful minority standing alone
bold witness against a rebellious crowd

schools.summary: Gill: they spake to all the company, to as many as could hear them, to the heads of the people, declaring the land an exceeding good land, against the evil report of the ten.

themes: The land, which we passed through to search it, is an exceeding good land
holding fast the true report
weighing the promise rather than the peril

schools.summary: Gill: the appeal to God's continued delight in them, as shown by all he had done in Egypt, at the Red sea, and in the wilderness.
Wesley: 'delight in us' — if by our rebellion and ingratitude we do not provoke God to leave and forsake us.

themes: If the LORD delight in us, then he will bring us into this land
the favour of God the ground of victory
the one danger their own unbelief

schools.summary: Gill: nothing could hinder their possessing the land but their own rebellion, which alone might provoke God to cut them off or deliver them to their enemies.
Clarke: 'their defence' is tsillam, 'their shadow,' a metaphor for protection; the shadow of God's defence over the Canaanites is departed, while the LORD is with Israel.

themes: Only rebel not ye against the LORD... their defence is departed from them, and the LORD is with us
the enemy stripped of their defence
unbelief the only real foe

schools.summary: Gill: the princes and heads bade stone Joshua and Caleb, who had made the faithful report and given the encouraging speech, the people being stirred up against them.
Henry: the glory of the LORD appeared in the tabernacle, interposing for his servants and against the rebels.

themes: all the congregation bade stone them... And the glory of the LORD appeared
a rescue of the faithful and rebuke to the rebels
to touch God's faithful is to face God

schools.summary: Gill: the LORD spake out of the cloud upon the tabernacle.
Henry: here is the righteous sentence God gave against Israel for their murmuring and unbelief, showing what their sin deserved and what would have been done had Moses not interposed.

themes: How long will this people provoke me? and how long will it be ere they believe me
the sin laid bare as unbelief
refusing trust against all the signs (Heb. 3:19)

schools.summary: Gill: as to the body of the people, this is not a peremptory decree but spoken partly by way of proposal to draw out what Moses would say, and partly as a threatening to bring the people to a sense of their sin and repentance.

themes: I will smite them with the pestilence, and disinherit them... make of thee a greater nation
a just sentence yet a proving of Moses
the offer of greatness testing his heart

schools.summary: Gill: Moses spake in an abrupt manner, his mind greatly disturbed and distressed by the threatening. Calvin: Moses, after his custom, stands 'in the breach' of the wall (Ps. 106:23) to avert the anger of God, supporting his request by argument.

themes: Then the Egyptians shall hear it... thou broughtest up this people in thy might
standing in the breach (Ps. 106:23)
pleading God's honour, not the people's worth

schools.summary: Gill: the Canaanites would hear of it, the nations being neighbours, and would tell it — with pleasure, as the Targum adds, that the Israelites were killed in the wilderness; the LORD was known to be among them in the cloud, seen face to face.

themes: they will tell it to the inhabitants... that thou LORD art among this people... seen face to face
the visible presence that honoured Israel
Moses' plea that God guard his name

schools.summary: Gill: 'kill all this people as one man' means suddenly and at once, as by a pestilence; the fame of the LORD had gone out among the nations, who would speak of so sudden a destruction.

themes: if thou shalt kill all this people as one man, then the nations... will speak
the fame of the LORD gone abroad
his glory turned to reproach if they perish

schools.summary: Gill: the heathen would conclude God, though he brought them out of Egypt, was not able to bring them through the wilderness into Canaan, his power having failed.
Wesley: 'not able' — his power was quite spent in bringing them out of Egypt, and could not finish the work, as the heathen would blasphemously say.

themes: the LORD was not able to bring this people into the land... therefore he hath slain them
the heathen ascribing it to impotence
God's power traduced, its honour Moses' plea

schools.summary: Gill: the power of God is already great, almighty, knowing no bounds; Moses' request is that it appear greater still in bringing the people into the land, magnified according to God's own word.

themes: let the power of my Lord be great, according as thou hast spoken
power magnified in pardoning, not destroying
the strength of mercy that bears with the sinful

schools.summary: Gill: the LORD is longsuffering toward all, and especially toward his own people; Calvin and Clarke refer to the fuller exposition at Exod. 34:6-7.

Wesley: even 'visiting the iniquity' / 'by no means clearing the guilty' is brought within the plea — Moses asks not that sin go unpunished but that the people be forgiven and chastened rather than destroyed.

themes: The LORD is longsuffering, and of great mercy, forgiving iniquity... by no means clearing the guilty
pleading God's name proclaimed at Sinai (Exod. 34:6-7)
the ground of pardon God's own character

schools.summary: Gill: the plain petition — pardon the iniquity of this people according to the greatness of thy mercy.

Clarke: Moses' intercession (vv.13-19) is full of simplicity and energy; his arguments are pointed, cogent, and respectful, showing a heart full of humanity and the deepest concern for the glory of God.

themes: Pardon, I beseech thee, the iniquity of this people according unto the greatness of thy mercy
the climax of a noble prayer
pleading God's long habit of forgiving

schools.summary: Gill: 'I have pardoned' means so as not to kill them utterly as one man — an instance of God's being plenteous in mercy and ready to forgive, and of the efficacy of the fervent prayer of a righteous man; the Jerusalem Targum's 'the Word of the LORD' Gill applies to Christ the essential Word.

themes: And the LORD said, I have pardoned according to thy word
the triumph of intercession
the plenteous mercy of God prevailed on by prayer

schools.summary: Gill: this is the form of an oath, the LORD swearing by his life, by himself, because he could swear by no greater.
Keil & Delitzsch: the oath 'as truly as I live, all the earth shall be filled with the glory of the LORD' is the ground of the sentence that follows on the rebels.

themes: as truly as I live, all the earth shall be filled with the glory of the LORD
God's purpose unfailing despite their unbelief
glory shining in judgment as in pardon (Hab. 2:14)

schools.summary: Gill: they had seen his glorious Majesty in the cloud and the glorious things he did from Egypt to the borders of Canaan; read 'that,' the matter of the oath; they tempted him ten times and hearkened not to his voice.
Keil & Delitzsch: all those men who had seen God's glory and miracles in Egypt and the wilderness had tempted him now these ten times.

themes: all those men which have seen my glory... have tempted me now these ten times
the full measure of their trying
sin aggravated by repetition against clear light

schools.summary: Gill: they should not possess the land the LORD had sworn to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob to give their seed; now he swears these men, who had tempted and disobeyed him, should not inherit it; the Targums take it for an oath.

themes: they shall not see the land which I sware unto their fathers... that provoked me
pardon joined with chastening
unbelief shutting them out of the rest (Heb. 3:19)

schools.summary: Gill: Caleb was one of the spies who brought a good and true report and stilled the people, approving himself a faithful servant; Joshua is not named here because, some say, he then had no children of whom it could be said the seed should possess the land; Caleb had 'another spirit' and followed the LORD fully.

themes: my servant Caleb, because he had another spirit with him, and hath followed me fully
the bright exception amid unbelief
whole-hearted following never in vain

schools.summary: Gill: the Canaanites (the Amorites) had come down from the mountain and sat in the valley, in ambush; Israel was now ordered to turn back into the wilderness by the way of the Red sea.

themes: To morrow turn you, and get you into the wilderness by the way of the Red sea
the door to Canaan shut
unbelief turning the road to rest into a wandering

schools.summary: Gill: before he had spoken to Moses alone, who interceded and obtained pardon; now he repeats to Moses and Aaron together that they should not possess the land.
Keil & Delitzsch: this is the sentence upon the murmuring congregation, after the Lord had pardoned the nation from utter destruction.

themes: the LORD spake unto Moses and unto Aaron
pardon and sentence now joined
grace sparing their being, justice on their sin

schools.summary: Gill: the LORD speaks as one weary of forbearing, their murmurings being so frequent and aggravated, asking how long sparing mercy must be extended; he had heard all their murmurings against him.

themes: How long shall I bear with this evil congregation, which murmur against me?
God weary of forbearing
long-tried mercy giving way to judgment

schools.summary: Gill: the form of an oath, 'as truly as I live.'
Keil & Delitzsch: Jehovah swore that it should happen to the murmurers as they had spoken in his ears.

themes: As truly as I live... as ye have spoken in mine ears, so will I do to you
their own words become their sentence
sin punished out of its own mouth

schools.summary: Gill: they had wished they had died in the wilderness, and the LORD now declares they should, their bodies falling there.
Keil & Delitzsch: the sentence fell on all numbered from twenty years old and upward who had murmured.

themes: Your carcases shall fall in this wilderness... from twenty years old and upward
the army that refused the land condemned
whose carcasses fell in the wilderness (Heb. 3:17)

schools.summary: Gill: read as an oath, 'I swear ye shall not'; save Caleb and Joshua.
Ellicott: 'concerning which I sware' is literally 'I lifted up my hand,' the attitude of swearing, referring to the covenant with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob (Gen. 14:22; Deut. 32:40).

themes: ye shall not come into the land... save Caleb... and Joshua
the oath of entry turned to exclusion
the promise kept to the believing, forfeited by the rest

schools.summary: Gill: these were the little ones the people had said should be a prey to the Canaanites; God would bring them in to know the land which their fathers had despised.

themes: your little ones, which ye said should be a prey, them will I bring in
the children's false fear answered in mercy
the promise standing for the next generation

schools.summary: Gill: the way of speaking distinguishes them from their children — 'as for you, your carcases.'
Wesley: see with what contempt they are spoken of, now they had by their sin made themselves vile.
Ellicott: 'in this wilderness' — the very words the Israelites themselves had used (v.2).

themes: But as for you, your carcases, they shall fall in this wilderness
the contemptuous word of judgment
their own murmur returned as their sentence (v.2)

schools.summary: Gill: 'wander' is 'feed,' like shepherds moving from place to place, the children grazing like sheep in the wilderness; the forty years reckoned from the coming out of Egypt; they must bear their fathers' whoredoms, their unfaithfulness to God.

themes: your children shall wander in the wilderness forty years, and bear your whoredoms
the innocent burdened by the fathers' sin
sin's consequences outlasting the sinners

schools.summary: Gill: the reckoning is a day for a year, forty days for forty years.

Clarke: the spies were forty days searching, and the people who rebelled on their evil report are condemned to forty years' wandering; God so hedged them that they could neither go back to Egypt nor forward to the promised land.

themes: forty days, each day for a year, shall ye bear your iniquities, even forty years
the punishment fitted to the sin
hedged in, neither back to Egypt nor forward

schools.summary: Gill: the decree is absolute and peremptory and will never be revoked, the LORD having determined and resolved it.
Keil & Delitzsch: as surely as Jehovah had spoken it, would he do it to that evil congregation, who should be consumed and die in the wilderness.

themes: I the LORD have said, I will surely do it unto all this evil congregation
the decree absolute and irreversible
abused patience passing into settled judgment

schools.summary: Gill: these were the ten spies, and only they at this time.
Keil & Delitzsch: the spies who had induced the congregation to revolt by their evil report were made a practical proof of the solemnity of the divine threatening.

themes: the men... who... made all the congregation to murmur... by bringing up a slander upon the land
the ringleaders marked for judgment
those who corrupt the many punished first

schools.summary: Gill: it was they, and they only at this time, who died.
Keil & Delitzsch: those men that brought up the evil report died by the plague before the LORD.

themes: those men that did bring up the evil report upon the land, died by the plague before the LORD
the ringleaders smitten at once
the earnest of the whole sentence

schools.summary: Gill: Joshua is here set first, as Caleb is elsewhere, showing they were equal in dignity.
Wesley: death never misses his mark, nor takes any by oversight who are designed for life, though in the midst of those that are to die.

themes: Joshua... and Caleb... which were of the men that went to search the land, lived still
the faithful spared amid the death of the ten
death taking none appointed to life

schools.summary: Gill: Moses told them all that murmured, of twenty years and upward, should die in the wilderness and never see Canaan.
Ellicott: their sorrow was for the punishment which their sin had entailed, not godly sorrow for the sin itself.

themes: Moses told these sayings... and the people mourned greatly
sorrow for the punishment, not the sin
worldly sorrow working no repentance (2 Cor. 7:10)

schools.summary: Gill: they could not sleep for the bad news, and were now in as great haste to go up and possess Canaan as they had been before to return to Egypt.
Keil & Delitzsch: they rose up early and gat them up into the top of the mountain, saying, 'Lo, we be here, and will go up.'

themes: they rose up early... and will go up unto the place which the LORD hath promised: for we have sinned
presumptuous haste, not repentance
the same disobedience in the dress of zeal

schools.summary: Gill: the command now was to turn back into the wilderness toward the Red sea, which they transgressed by going forward. Calvin: he rejects this feigned penitence, by which sinners 'twist round the pot,' trying all shifts rather than truly submit to God.

themes: Wherefore now do ye transgress the commandment of the LORD? but it shall not prosper
obedience out of season failing
feigned penitence, self-will dressed as devotion

schools.summary: Gill: victory is of the Lord; the Targum adds that the ark, tabernacle, and cloud of glory did not move — a plain sign God would not go with them, and they could not hope to prevail.
Ellicott: go not up, for the LORD is not among you.

themes: Go not up, for the LORD is not among you; that ye be not smitten
the symbols of his presence unmoved
presence and victory standing or falling together

schools.summary: Gill: the Amalekites and Canaanites were there before them, having removed or detached a party to hold the pass on the top of the mountain.
Ellicott: 'are there before you' — they had left their tents and were waiting; because Israel turned from the LORD, he would not be with them.

themes: the Amalekites and the Canaanites are there before you, and ye shall fall... because ye are turned away from the LORD
the true cause of defeat the absence of God
forsaking God is to fall before the least foe

schools.summary: Gill: they went up in a bold, audacious, presumptuous manner, withdrawing from God and his minister, lifted up in themselves and confident of their own strength; the Vulgate reads 'being darkened they went up'; the ark of the covenant and Moses departed not out of the camp.

themes: they presumed to go up unto the hill top... the ark... and Moses, departed not out of the camp
venturing in their own strength against the warning
the picture of presumption marching to ruin

schools.summary: Gill: the enemies, having the advantage of the higher ground, came down upon the Israelites and smote them, discomfiting them even unto Hormah, the place so named from this destruction.

themes: the Amalekites came down, and the Canaanites... smote them, and discomfited them, even unto Hormah
the presumed victory turned to rout
presumption ending in defeat, the place of pride become ruin
